name: mac-automation
description: This skill should be used when the user requests Mac automation via AppleScript, including: Mail operations, Reminders/Calendar management, Safari control, Finder operations, system controls (volume, brightness, notifications, app launching), third-party apps (development tools, task management, media players), or mentions \"AppleScript\" or \"automate Mac\".
capabilities: Send and manage emails through Mail app with subject, body, and attachments
Create and manage reminders with due dates and priority levels
Create calendar events with times, locations, and recurring schedules
Control Safari tabs, open URLs, and execute JavaScript on web pages
Manage files and folders via Finder operations like open, move, copy
Adjust volume, display notifications, and control system settings
author: 7Sageer, Claude
securitySummary: Legitimate macOS automation skill using standard osascript command for AppleScript execution. Static scanner flagged benign patterns including keystroke commands (false positive - these simulate keyboard input, not capture it), screenshot commands (false positive - local saves only), and crypt keywords (false positive - scanner misidentified screencapture/caffeinate command names). All capabilities align with stated purpose of controlling Mail, Calendar, Reminders, Safari, Finder, and System Events applications. User-initiated operations with confirmation requirements for destructive actions documented.
valueStatement: Control macOS applications and system functions through natural language commands. Send emails, manage reminders, open websites, adjust volume, and more without leaving your AI assistant.
limitations: Requires macOS with accessibility and automation permissions granted
Only works with applications that support AppleScript natively
Cannot automate apps without proper macOS permissions configured

useCases.title: Quick daily briefing
useCases.description: Get your daily calendar events and unread emails in seconds without opening multiple apps.

useCases.title: Create reminders from text
useCases.description: Turn clipboard content or conversation text into reminders with due dates automatically.

useCases.title: Browser and file control
useCases.description: Open URLs, manage tabs, and organize files through simple text commands.

promptTemplates.title: Send email
promptTemplates.prompt: Send an email to [recipient] with subject [subject] and body [message content].

promptTemplates.title: Create reminder
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a reminder called [task name] due [date/time] in my [list name] list.

promptTemplates.title: Add calendar event
promptTemplates.prompt: Add a calendar event for [event name] on [date] at [time] at [location].

promptTemplates.title: Morning briefing
promptTemplates.prompt: Give me a briefing of my calendar for today and list my unread emails from Mail.

bestPractices: Grant automation permissions in System Preferences before first use for each application
Confirm destructive operations like delete or empty trash before executing
Use exact application names like Mail, Calendar, Reminders, Safari, Finder
antiPatterns: Running automation without checking application permissions first
Executing delete commands without user confirmation
Assuming all third-party applications support AppleScript

faq.question: Which macOS versions are supported?
faq.answer: Requires macOS with AppleScript support (10.4+). Most features work on modern macOS versions including Ventura, Sonoma, and Sequoia.

faq.question: What are the permission limits?
faq.answer: Each application requires explicit Automation permission in System Preferences > Privacy and Security. First runs trigger macOS permission dialogs.

faq.question: Can this integrate with other tools?
faq.answer: Yes, works with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. Can chain with other Bash commands and MCP tools for complex workflows.

faq.question: Is my data safe?
faq.answer: All operations run locally on your Mac via osascript. No data is sent externally. You control what information is accessed.

faq.question: Why did my script fail?
faq.answer: Common causes: app not running, incorrect app name, missing permissions, or syntax error. Check System Preferences permissions and ensure app is installed.

faq.question: How does this compare to Shortcuts?
faq.answer: AppleScript offers deeper control over application internals. Shortcuts provides visual workflow design. Use AppleScript for app-specific automation requiring precise control.
